text: Three-arm relay three is an even harder iteration of the three-arm relay exercises.
The big difference in this is that you are now required to place shapes in panels that are both flat and at a vertical orientation.
Additionally, the shapes and placement of panels is randomly generated and the order is not always the same as you go through each attempt.

Again, we're sticking with the coordination of movements where the outer left and outer right arms are doing the door opening and the middle arm is performing the removal and placement of the designated shape.
There will be a tendency to want to move quickly through this exercise as there are a lot of panels.
However, patience and taking your time are key, specifically when it comes to the three arm relay coordination and the swapping between the middle and right arm.
The smaller and more defined movements that you can do, as well as the continual camera control and camera view changes, will really allow you to do this exercise the most efficiently.
Furthermore, avid use of the clutch in this exercise is essential to moving between panels and to proper placement of the shapes into their designated spots.
The robotic system zooms out, and all three arms make a coordinated global movement towards the blue panel located on the screen's left side.
The surgeon adjusts the left and right outer robotic arms to grasp the door handles, demonstrating precise control of the robotic instruments.

For instance, on the left side of the exercise, we will grab the bottom door handle with the left arm and the top door handle with the right arm, whereas when we were on the right side of the exercise with the red panel, we actually used the right arm on the bottom door handle and the left arm on the top door handle, which is the exact opposite we see here with the blue panel.
The operator attempts to place a blue shape into its designated receptacle, encountering difficulty due to suboptimal grip from the previous panel.
The surgeon demonstrates grabbing the shapes in a vertical orientation, focusing on holding them at the edge of the stem for optimal placement.
This gives you the most distance and the best range of motion to place these in their proper receptacle without causing any issues.
The flat panels provide the easiest access to the shapes and so grabbing them in that orientation, vertical and as far away from the stem edge as possible.
The orange shape is placed into the orange receptacle, demonstrating a smooth insertion process.
Make sure to keep all three instruments in your camera view at all times.
The surgeon receives a warning sign indicating the right arm is moving out of view, prompting a quick camera adjustment to maintain visibility of all instruments.
And a reminder again that patience is key with this exercise.
The surgeon makes multiple camera adjustments and clutches, swapping the robotic arms to reposition before attempting to open the green panel.
A final note on excessive force.
This exercise is more sensitive than the other three arm exercises to over excessive pulling on the door arms, so keep that in mind.
